title: Can machine learning for quantum-gas experiments be explainable?
authors: I. B. Spielman amd J. P. Zwolak
abstract: Virtually all aspects of many-body atomic physics are challenging: experiments are technically demanding, datasets have become enormous, and the memory and CPU requirements for classical simulation of generic quantum systems often scale exponentially with system size. Machine learning (ML) methods are already assisting in each of these areas and are poised to become transformative. Here, we focus on two specific applications of ML to cold-atom-based quantum simulators. These devices generally generate data in the form of images; we first showcase denoising of raw images and then identify solitonic waves in Bose-Einstein condensates. In both of these examples, we comment on the interplay between performance, model complexity, and interpretability.
summary: This paper investigates whether machine learning for cold-atom experiments can be both accurate and explainable. By comparing CNNs to Explainable Boosting Machines, the authors show that physics-informed, interpretable models can effectively classify quantum gas excitations like solitons and vortices.
main_problem: The challenge of applying machine learning to quantum-gas experiments while balancing high performance with scientific interpretability and avoiding the 'black-box' memorization of imaging artifacts.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Explainable Boosting Machines (EBMs) can match or surpass CNN performance in classifying excitations like solitons and vortices, providing human-readable, physics-aligned explanations.
method: The study compares traditional linear algebra, CNNs (U-Nets, ResNet), and Explainable Boosting Machines (EBMs) applied to image denoising and feature classification.
model_or_system: The physical platform consists of neutral-atom quantum simulators, specifically Bose-Einstein Condensates (BECs), using bright-field imaging (absorption or phase-contrast) to detect solitons and vortices.
key_observables: Atomic density (optical depth), soliton position, velocity, depth, and width, and vortex vorticity.
important_parameters: Probe wavelength, resonant absorption cross-section, and photon shot noise levels.
main_assumptions: The signal of interest is assumed to be concentrated within a specific interior region of the imaging window.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the imaging system and comparison of reconstruction methods; Figure 2 illustrates PCA eigenvalue spectra and templates; Figure 4 shows the ResNet architecture; Figure 10/11 display excitation classes and EBM feature importance/shape functions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the imaging challenges, compares linear algebra vs. ML for reference reconstruction, evaluates CNNs vs. EBMs for excitation classification, and concludes on the feasibility of explainable scientific ML.
why_interesting: It addresses a critical bottleneck in many-body dynamics experiments: how to use powerful ML tools to extract physical signals from noisy, high-volume data without losing the ability to verify the underlying physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 4. ResNet encoder-decoder. Data-flow is indicated by lines, while dimension-changing layers are denoted by trape- zoids. Functional groups are denoted by larger rectangles.

FIG. 5. ResNet training with 8-fold cross-validation. Train- ing and validation data are denoted by solid and dashed curves, respectively, and include 1-sigma error bands. The inset zooms in on the fine-tuning stage of training, and the markers show the results for the linear models in Fig. 2 for conventional PCA (blue) and optimal linear (red).

FIG. 6. Images showing evolution of the BEC (measured after TOF) from 1 ms (panel A) to 10 ms (panel E) after imprinting a phase step of about 1.5π on the top half of the condensate. A positive density disturbance is moving rapidly in the +x direction, while a dark solitonic excitation (most likely a vortex ring) is moving significantly slower in the −x direction (opposite to the direction of the applied force).
